title: Evaluating Japan’s New Grand Strategy
gist: IISS landing page for Robert Ward's Adelphi book assessing whether Japan's 2022 security overhaul is robust enough to secure the country in a Taiwan-contingency scenario, against intensifying China assertiveness, China-Russia and Russia-North Korea alignments, and a recalibrating Trump-administration US posture. The page is promotional but states several concrete strategic claims.

insights.claim: Japan's grand strategy now rests on its 2022 National Security Strategy, National Defense Strategy and Defense Buildup Program, and IISS treats meeting these goals as a question of global importance.
insights.speaker: Robert Ward
insights.so_what: Frames Japan's multi-year defense build-out as a structural, documented commitment — supportive of Japanese defense-industrial and dual-use suppliers and of allied procurement demand.
insights.quote: whether Japan can meet the grand-strategic goals that it established in its 2022 National Security Strategy, National Defense Strategy and Defense Buildup Program
insights.locator: As such, it is of global importance

insights.claim: The explicit benchmark for Japan's new strategy is sufficiency 'even in the scenario of a Chinese attack on Taiwan' — IISS makes a Taiwan contingency the stress-test for Japanese rearmament.
insights.speaker: Robert Ward
insights.so_what: Taiwan-contingency planning is the binding constraint on Japanese force posture; a watch-item for escalation pricing across Asian risk assets and shipping/insurance.
insights.quote: Japan’s goal of ensuring its security even in the scenario of a Chinese attack on Taiwan

insights.claim: IISS attributes intensifying Indo-Pacific stress to three compounding drivers: China's more assertive behaviour, China-Russia and Russia-North Korea alignments, and a recalibration of US foreign policy under the second Trump administration.
insights.speaker: Robert Ward
insights.so_what: A concise driver-map for Asian geopolitical risk; the cited US recalibration is the key swing variable for allied burden-sharing and regional hedging.
insights.quote: from the apparent recalibration of United States foreign policy under the second Trump administration

insights.claim: IISS frames Japan as a rising independent strategic actor whose significance now extends 'both in the Indo-Pacific and beyond,' not merely a US dependent.
insights.speaker: Robert Ward
insights.so_what: Reinforces the structural case for Japan as a growing autonomous defense buyer and security partner, beyond US alliance spend.
insights.quote: magnified Japan’s significance as a strategic actor both in the Indo-Pacific and beyond
